tests.condition: Rule-out test in low / intermediate pre-test probability

citation.reference: Patel P, et al. Systematic review and meta-analysis of test accuracy for the diagnosis of suspected pulmonary embolism. Blood Adv. 2020;4(18):4296-4311 (61 studies).
citation.sourceQuote: The pooled estimates for D-dimer sensitivity and specificity were 0.97 (95% confidence interval [CI], 0.96-0.98) and 0.41 (95% CI, 0.36-0.46) respectively.
citation.context: Quantitative D-dimer; patients with suspected first/recurrent PE. Values vary by assay (ELISA vs latex vs whole-blood) and increase in specificity with age-adjusted thresholds.
citation.notes: High sensitivity / low specificity makes a NEGATIVE result a strong rule-out (low LR-). DOI/PMID must be re-verified by faculty before teaching use.

tests.condition: Confirmatory imaging, typically after a positive D-dimer or high pre-test probability

citation.reference: Patel P, et al. Systematic review and meta-analysis of test accuracy for the diagnosis of suspected pulmonary embolism. Blood Adv. 2020;4(18):4296-4311 (61 studies).
citation.sourceQuote: CTPA sensitivity and specificity were 0.94 (95% CI, 0.89-0.97) and 0.98 (95% CI, 0.97-0.99), respectively.
citation.context: CT pulmonary angiography in suspected PE; performance depends on scanner generation and image quality.
citation.notes: High specificity makes a POSITIVE result strongly rule-in (high LR+).

tests.condition: Indirect test for PE via detection of proximal DVT

citation.reference: Patel P, et al. Systematic review and meta-analysis of test accuracy for the diagnosis of suspected pulmonary embolism. Blood Adv. 2020;4(18):4296-4311 (61 studies).
citation.sourceQuote: CUS sensitivity and specificity were 0.49 (95% CI, 0.31-0.66) and 0.96 (95% CI, 0.95-0.98), respectively.
citation.context: Compression ultrasonography detects proximal DVT as a surrogate for PE; low sensitivity means a negative study does NOT exclude PE.
citation.notes: Illustrates a rule-in (not rule-out) test: high specificity, low sensitivity.

tests.condition: Suspected lower-extremity DVT

citation.reference: Bhatt M, et al. Diagnosis of deep vein thrombosis of the lower extremity: a systematic review and meta-analysis of test accuracy. Blood Adv. 2020;4(7):1250-1264 (43 studies).
citation.sourceQuote: For any suspected DVT, the pooled estimates for sensitivity and specificity of proximal compression US were 90.1% (95% confidence interval [CI], 86.5-92.8) and 98.5% (95% CI, 97.6-99.1), respectively.
citation.context: Proximal (not whole-leg) compression ultrasonography for lower-extremity DVT; whole-leg and serial US have different characteristics.
citation.notes: High specificity makes a POSITIVE result a strong rule-in. Verify against source before teaching use.

tests.condition: Rule-out in low / intermediate pre-test probability

citation.reference: Bhatt M, et al. Diagnosis of deep vein thrombosis of the lower extremity: a systematic review and meta-analysis of test accuracy. Blood Adv. 2020;4(7):1250-1264 (43 studies).
citation.sourceQuote: For D-dimer, pooled estimates were 96.1% (95% CI, 92.6-98.0) and 35.7% (95% CI, 29.5-42.4).
citation.context: Quantitative D-dimer for suspected lower-extremity DVT; low specificity means a positive result is non-specific.
citation.notes: High sensitivity / low specificity → strong rule-out when negative.

tests.condition: Suspected heart failure (optimal cutpoint)

citation.reference: Booth RA, et al. Performance of BNP and NT-proBNP for diagnosis of heart failure in primary care patients: a systematic review. Heart Fail Rev. 2014;19(4):439-451.
citation.sourceQuote: Pooled sensitivity and specificity of BNP and NT-proBNP using the lowest [0.85 (sensitivity) and 0.54 (specificity)], optimal (0.80 and 0.61), and manufacturers' (0.74 and 0.67) cutpoints showed good performance for diagnosing HF.
citation.context: Primary-care populations; performance is cutpoint-dependent (values shown are the 'optimal' cutpoint). Specificity is lower and more variable than sensitivity.
citation.notes: Best used as a rule-out (high sensitivity at low thresholds). Acute-care thresholds differ; verify before teaching use.

tests.condition: Suspected heart failure (optimal cutpoint)

citation.reference: Booth RA, et al. Performance of BNP and NT-proBNP for diagnosis of heart failure in primary care patients: a systematic review. Heart Fail Rev. 2014;19(4):439-451.
citation.sourceQuote: Similar performance was seen for NT-proBNP: lowest (0.90 and 0.50), optimal (0.86 and 0.58), and manufacturers' (0.82 and 0.58) cutpoints.
citation.context: Primary-care populations; 'optimal' cutpoint shown. No statistically significant difference in accuracy vs BNP.
citation.notes: Rule-out test; threshold- and population-dependent. Verify before teaching use.

tests.condition: ED patients with suspected ACS without ST-elevation; serial 0h/1h algorithm

citation.reference: Burgos LM, et al. Performance of the ESC 0/1-hour algorithm in the diagnosis of myocardial infarction with high-sensitivity cardiac troponin: systematic review and meta-analysis. Eur Heart J Acute Cardiovasc Care. 2020;10(3):279-286 (11 studies, 19,213 patients).
citation.sourceQuote: Summary sensitivity and specificity in diagnosing AMI were 99% (95% CI 98-99%;63%) and 91% (95% CI 91-92%;96%) respectively.
citation.context: Emergency-department suspected ACS, AMI prevalence ~11%; the 0h/1h SERIAL hs-cTn algorithm, not a single draw. The 63% / 96% after each CI are I-squared heterogeneity, not part of the interval.
citation.notes: Very high sensitivity → excellent rule-out; the algorithm also rules in. Verify before teaching use.

tests.condition: Children with pharyngitis (vs throat culture)

citation.reference: Cohen JF, et al. Rapid antigen detection test for group A streptococcus in children with pharyngitis. Cochrane Database Syst Rev. 2016;7:CD010502 (105 evaluations, 58,244 participants).
citation.sourceQuote: RADT had a summary sensitivity of 85.6%; 95% confidence interval (CI) 83.3 to 87.6 and a summary specificity of 95.4%; 95% CI 94.5 to 96.2.
citation.context: Children with pharyngitis, RADT vs throat culture; median GAS prevalence ~29.5%. Sensitivity is heterogeneous across studies.
citation.notes: High specificity (rule-in); imperfect sensitivity means a negative RADT may warrant culture in children. Verify before teaching use.

tests.condition: Adults, secondary care (vs duodenal biopsy)

citation.reference: Sheppard AL, et al. Systematic review with meta-analysis: the accuracy of serological tests to support the diagnosis of coeliac disease. Aliment Pharmacol Ther. 2022;55(5):514-527.
citation.sourceQuote: Summary sensitivity and specificity of immunoglobulin A (IgA) anti-tissue transglutaminase were 90.7% (95% confidence interval: 87.3%, 93.2%) and 87.4% (84.4%, 90.0%) in adults (5 studies).
citation.context: Adults in secondary care vs duodenal biopsy; values differ in children (sens ~97.7%, spec ~70.2%). Requires adequate gluten intake and total-IgA sufficiency.
citation.notes: Adult values shown; pediatric characteristics differ. Verify before teaching use.

tests.condition: Known or suspected rheumatoid arthritis

citation.reference: Zhang WC, Wu H, Chen WX. Meta-analysis: diagnostic accuracy of anti-CCP-2 and anti-CCP-3 antibody in rheumatoid arthritis. Clin Chem Lab Med. 2014;52(6):779-790 (17 studies).
citation.context: Anti-CCP-2 assay for known/suspected RA. High specificity, moderate sensitivity.
citation.notes: Classic rule-in test (high specificity); a negative result does not exclude RA. Verify before teaching use.

tests.condition: Suspected influenza A (vs RT-PCR)

citation.reference: Merckx J, et al. Diagnostic accuracy of novel and traditional rapid tests for influenza infection compared with RT-PCR: a systematic review and meta-analysis. Ann Intern Med. 2017;167(6):394-409 (162 studies).
citation.sourceQuote: Pooled sensitivities for detecting influenza A ... were 54.4% (95% credible interval [CrI], 48.9% to 59.8%) for RIDTs ... Pooled specificities were uniformly high (>98%).
citation.context: Traditional RIDT vs RT-PCR, influenza A. Specificity uniformly >98% (0.98 used as the stated floor; no precise spec interval reported). Sensitivity is higher in children and far higher for digital immunoassays and rapid NAATs.
citation.notes: The textbook low-sensitivity / high-specificity test: a POSITIVE rules in, but a NEGATIVE does NOT rule out influenza. Sensitivity CrI 48.9-59.8%. Verify before teaching use.

tests.condition: Suspected PPGL (typically a low-prevalence screened population)

citation.reference: Chen Y, et al. Accuracy of plasma free metanephrines in the diagnosis of pheochromocytoma and paraganglioma: a systematic review and meta-analysis. Endocr Pract. 2017;23(10):1169-1177.
citation.sourceQuote: NMN was generally more accurate than individual tests, with the highest AUC (0.99, 95% CI 0.97-0.99), DOR (443.35, 95% CI 216.9-906.23), and pooled sensitivity (0.97, 95% CI 0.94-0.98) values.
citation.context: Plasma free normetanephrine (NMN) for PPGL; per the same meta-analysis NMN specificity was 0.97 (95% CI 0.92-0.99). LC-MS/MS preferred; cutoff and sampling position drive heterogeneity.
citation.notes: Teaching case: even at ~97% sensitivity AND specificity, the low pre-test probability of PPGL keeps PPV modest. Verify before teaching use.

tests.condition: Suspected SLE (vs systemic rheumatic disease and healthy controls)

citation.reference: Jeong S, et al. Diagnostic value of screening enzyme immunoassays compared to indirect immunofluorescence for anti-nuclear antibodies in patients with systemic rheumatic diseases: a systematic review and meta-analysis. Semin Arthritis Rheum. 2018;48(2):334-342.
citation.sourceQuote: the summary sensitivities of SEIA vs. IIF were 87.4% vs 88.4% for combined SRDs, 89.4% vs. 95.2% for SLE ... the summary specificities of SEIA vs. IIF were 79.7% vs.78.9% for combined SRDs, 89.1% vs. 83.3% for SLE
citation.context: ANA by indirect immunofluorescence (IIF) for SLE. High sensitivity → good rule-out; specificity is limited because ANA is positive in many other conditions and some healthy people. Screening EIA differs (sens 89.4%, spec 89.1%).
citation.notes: Classic rule-out: a negative ANA makes SLE unlikely; a positive ANA is non-specific. Verify before teaching use.

tests.condition: Adults suspected of infection / sepsis (vs blood cultures)

citation.reference: Hoeboer SH, et al. The diagnostic accuracy of procalcitonin for bacteraemia: a systematic review and meta-analysis. Clin Microbiol Infect. 2015;21(5):474-481 (58 studies, 16,514 patients).
citation.sourceQuote: The optimal and most widely used procalcitonin cut-off value was 0.5 ng/mL with a corresponding sensitivity of 76% and specificity of 69%.
citation.context: Procalcitonin at 0.5 ng/mL for bacteraemia (positive blood cultures) in adults; overall SROC AUC 0.79; accuracy varies by subgroup (ICU higher, neutropenic lower).
citation.notes: Moderate sensitivity AND specificity — a good example of a test that only modestly shifts probability. Verify before teaching use.

tests.condition: Adults with presumptive pulmonary TB (vs culture)

citation.reference: Horne DJ, et al. Xpert MTB/RIF and Xpert MTB/RIF Ultra for pulmonary tuberculosis and rifampicin resistance in adults. Cochrane Database Syst Rev. 2019;6:CD009593 (86 studies, 42,091 participants).
citation.sourceQuote: Xpert MTB/RIF pooled sensitivity and specificity (95% credible Interval (CrI)) were 85% (82% to 88%) and 98% (97% to 98%), (70 studies, 37,237 unselected participants; high-certainty evidence).
citation.context: Adults with presumptive pulmonary TB vs culture. Sensitivity is lower in smear-negative (67%) and HIV-positive (81%) patients, so a negative result does not exclude TB.
citation.notes: High specificity → strong rule-in; imperfect sensitivity (especially smear-negative/HIV+) means negatives need follow-up. Verify before teaching use.

tests.condition: Suspected giant cell (temporal) arteritis (vs temporal artery biopsy)

citation.reference: Ball EL, et al. Role of ultrasonography in the diagnosis of temporal arteritis. Br J Surg. 2010;97(12):1765-1771 (17 studies, 998 patients).
citation.sourceQuote: When the halo sign on duplex imaging was compared with TA biopsy, the sensitivity was 75 (95 per cent confidence interval 67 to 82) per cent and the specificity was 83 (78 to 88) per cent.
citation.context: Halo sign on colour duplex ultrasonography vs temporal artery biopsy. Newer studies and protocols (e.g. axillary involvement, compression sign) may differ.
citation.notes: Imperfect on both axes; a negative scan does not exclude GCA when suspicion is high. Verify before teaching use.

tests.condition: Suspected acute aortic syndrome (rule-out)

citation.reference: Watanabe H, et al. Diagnostic test accuracy of D-dimer for acute aortic syndrome: systematic review and meta-analysis of 22 studies with 5000 subjects. Sci Rep. 2016;6:26893.
citation.sourceQuote: Based on ... 12 studies that used the cutoff value of 500 ng/ml, the sensitivity was 0.952 (95% CI 0.901-0.978), the specificity was 0.604 (95% CI 0.485-0.712).
citation.context: Acute aortic syndrome/dissection, D-dimer cutoff 500 ng/mL. High sensitivity, low specificity. Pre-test probability scoring (e.g. ADD-RS) modifies how it is used.
citation.notes: Rule-out parallel to D-dimer in PE: a value <500 ng/mL substantially lowers the probability of dissection. Verify before teaching use.

tests.condition: Suspected H. pylori, off PPIs/antibiotics (vs histopathology)

citation.reference: Best LMJ, et al. Non-invasive diagnostic tests for Helicobacter pylori infection. Cochrane Database Syst Rev. 2018;3:CD012080 (101 studies, 11,003 participants).
citation.sourceQuote: The sensitivity (95% CI) estimated at a fixed specificity of 0.90 (median from studies across the four tests), was 0.94 (95% CI 0.89 to 0.97) for urea breath test-C
citation.context: 13C urea breath test vs histopathology; sensitivity estimated at a FIXED specificity of 0.90 (the cross-test median, so no separate specificity CI). Recent PPIs or antibiotics reduce sensitivity.
citation.notes: Sensitivity 95% CI 0.89-0.97. The most accurate non-invasive test; serology and stool antigen are somewhat less accurate. Verify before teaching use.

tests.condition: Differentiating bacterial from aseptic meningitis

citation.reference: Sakushima K, et al. Diagnostic accuracy of cerebrospinal fluid lactate for differentiating bacterial meningitis from aseptic meningitis: a meta-analysis. J Infect. 2011;62(4):255-262 (33 studies).
citation.sourceQuote: The pooled test characteristics of CSF lactate were sensitivity 0.93 (95% CI: 0.89-0.96), specificity 0.96 (95% CI: 0.93-0.98), likelihood ratio positive 22.9 ...
citation.context: CSF lactate to distinguish bacterial from aseptic meningitis, cutoff ~35 mg/dL. CRITICAL caveat: prior antibiotic treatment lowers sensitivity to ~0.49.
citation.notes: Strong test in the untreated patient, but pre-treatment with antibiotics markedly reduces sensitivity. Verify before teaching use.

tests.condition: ED patients with acute dyspnea (point-of-care)

citation.reference: Al Deeb M, et al. Point-of-care ultrasonography for the diagnosis of acute cardiogenic pulmonary edema in patients presenting with acute dyspnea: a systematic review and meta-analysis. Acad Emerg Med. 2014;21(8):843-852 (7 studies, 1075 patients).
citation.sourceQuote: The sensitivity of US using B-lines to diagnosis ACPE is 94.1% (95% confidence interval [CI] = 81.3% to 98.3%) and the specificity is 92.4% (95% CI = 84.2% to 96.4%).
citation.context: Point-of-care lung ultrasound B-lines for acute cardiogenic pulmonary edema in undifferentiated dyspnea; reference standard was final clinical diagnosis.
citation.notes: Good rule-in and rule-out at the bedside; operator- and protocol-dependent. Verify before teaching use.

tests.condition: Adults with suspected appendicitis (add-on after clinical evaluation)

citation.reference: Rud B, et al. Computed tomography for diagnosis of acute appendicitis in adults. Cochrane Database Syst Rev. 2019;(11):CD009977 (64 studies, 10,280 participants).
citation.sourceQuote: Summary sensitivity was 0.95 (95% confidence interval (CI) 0.93 to 0.96), and summary specificity was 0.94 (95% CI 0.92 to 0.95).
citation.context: Adults with suspected appendicitis, CT vs surgical/follow-up reference; median prevalence 0.43. IV/contrast-enhanced CT slightly more sensitive than unenhanced.
citation.notes: High on both axes → strong rule-in and rule-out. Verify before teaching use.

tests.condition: Distinguishing IBD from functional GI disorders (e.g. IBS)

citation.reference: An YK, et al. Faecal calprotectin testing for identifying patients with organic gastrointestinal disease: systematic review and meta-analysis. Med J Aust. 2019;211(10):461-467 (18 studies).
citation.sourceQuote: For distinguishing IBD from functional GIDs (ten studies), sensitivity was 88% (95% CI, 80-93%), specificity 72% (95% CI, 59-82%), and AUC 0.89.
citation.context: Fecal calprotectin (ELISA, cutoff 50-100 µg/g) to separate IBD from functional GI disorders. The same review notes a very high negative predictive value at low prevalence.
citation.notes: Excellent rule-out at low prevalence (good triage before colonoscopy); modest specificity (raised in many organic conditions). Verify before teaching use.

tests.condition: Immunocompromised / neutropenic patients (vs EORTC/MSG criteria)

citation.reference: Leeflang MMG, et al. Galactomannan detection for invasive aspergillosis in immunocompromised patients. Cochrane Database Syst Rev. 2015;(12):CD007394 (54 studies, 5660 patients).
citation.sourceQuote: When using an optical density index (ODI) of 0.5 as a cut-off value, the sensitivity of the test was 82% (73% to 90%) and the specificity was 81% (72% to 90%).
citation.context: Serum galactomannan ELISA in neutropenic/immunocompromised patients; cutoff ODI 0.5. Higher cutoffs (1.0, 1.5) raise specificity at the cost of sensitivity. Results were heterogeneous.
citation.notes: Threshold choice trades sensitivity for specificity — a good cutoff-effect teaching example. Verify before teaching use.

tests.condition: Screening previously undiagnosed adults (vs OGTT)

citation.reference: Kaur G, et al. Diagnostic accuracy of tests for type 2 diabetes and prediabetes: a systematic review and meta-analysis. PLoS One. 2020;15(11):e0242415 (HbA1c: 17 studies).
citation.sourceQuote: The pooled sensitivity, specificity, positive (LR+) and negative likelihood ratio (LR-) for diagnosing diabetes with HbA1c (6.5%; venous sample; n = 17 studies) were 50% (95% CI: 42-59%), 97.3% (95% CI: 95.3-98.4), 18.32 (95% CI: 11.06-30.53) and 0.51 (95% CI: 0.43-0.60), respectively.
citation.context: HbA1c at the 6.5% diagnostic threshold vs OGTT in previously undiagnosed community adults. A lower cutoff (~6.0%) raises sensitivity at the cost of specificity.
citation.notes: Striking teaching case: at 6.5%, HbA1c is highly specific (rules in) but misses ~half of OGTT-defined diabetes — a normal value does NOT exclude it. Verify before teaching use.

tests.testName: Serum lipase (>3x upper limit of normal)
tests.condition: ED patients with acute abdominal pain

citation.reference: Rompianesi G, et al. Serum amylase and lipase and urinary trypsinogen and amylase for diagnosis of acute pancreatitis. Cochrane Database Syst Rev. 2017;(4):CD012010 (10 studies).
citation.sourceQuote: ...sensitivities (0.72 (95% CI 0.59 to 0.82); 0.79 (95% CI 0.54 to 0.92); and 0.72 (95% CI 0.56 to 0.84), respectively) and specificities (0.93 (95% CI 0.66 to 0.99); 0.89 (95% CI 0.46 to 0.99); and 0.90 (95% CI 0.85 to 0.93), respectively).
citation.context: Serum lipase at >3x ULN for acute pancreatitis in ED abdominal pain (the middle value of each parenthetical list is lipase). Data were sparse — note the very wide CIs; accuracy declines with time from onset.
citation.notes: ~1 in 4 cases missed even with lipase >3x ULN; keep a low threshold to treat if symptoms fit. Verify before teaching use.

tests.condition: Staging F4 fibrosis in chronic hepatitis B (vs liver biopsy)

citation.reference: Chon YE, et al. Performance of transient elastography for the staging of liver fibrosis in patients with chronic hepatitis B: a meta-analysis. PLoS One. 2012;7(9):e44930 (18 studies, 2772 patients).
citation.sourceQuote: The cutoff value for F4 was 11.7 (range, 7.3-17.5) kPa, with a sensitivity of 84.6% and specificity of 81.5%.
citation.context: Transient elastography (FibroScan) for cirrhosis (F4) in chronic hepatitis B, cutoff ~11.7 kPa. Optimal cutoffs differ by etiology (hepatitis C, NAFLD).
citation.notes: Non-invasive alternative to biopsy; cutoff- and etiology-dependent. Verify before teaching use.

tests.condition: Screening adults for Cushing syndrome

citation.reference: Galm BP, et al. Accuracy of laboratory tests for the diagnosis of Cushing syndrome. J Clin Endocrinol Metab. 2020;105(6):dgaa105 (139 studies, 14,140 participants).
citation.context: Overnight 1-mg dexamethasone suppression test for Cushing syndrome (the values are sensitivity, then specificity, then LR+ and LR-). Late-night salivary cortisol and 24h urinary free cortisol have similar accuracy.
citation.notes: Very high sensitivity → excellent first-line rule-out; false positives occur (estrogen, CYP3A4 inducers, etc.). Verify before teaching use.

tests.condition: Screening hypertensive patients for primary aldosteronism

citation.reference: Li X, et al. Aldosterone/direct renin concentration ratio as a screening test for primary aldosteronism: a meta-analysis. J Renin Angiotensin Aldosterone Syst. 2016;17(3) (9 studies, 974 patients).
citation.sourceQuote: The overall sensitivity, specificity, area under the curve, and diagnostic odds ratio of ADRR were 0.89 (95% confidence interval (CI) 0.84-0.93), 0.96 (95% CI 0.95-0.98), 0.985 and 324 respectively, with substantial heterogeneity.
citation.context: Aldosterone/renin ratio screening for primary aldosteronism. CRITICAL: antihypertensive drugs interfere; off-treatment accuracy is higher (sens 0.99, spec 0.98).
citation.notes: Screening test; confirm positives with dynamic testing. Antihypertensives must be accounted for. Verify before teaching use.

tests.condition: Lymphocytic pleural effusion (TB-endemic settings)

citation.reference: Palma RM, et al. Diagnostic accuracy of pleural fluid adenosine deaminase for diagnosing tuberculosis. Arch Bronconeumol. 2019;55(1):23-30.
citation.sourceQuote: In 73 studies from non-Spanish populations a trend toward lower ADA sensitivity (88%, 95% CI:86%-90%) and specificity (88%, 95% CI: 86%-90%) was noted
citation.context: Pleural fluid ADA for tuberculous pleuritis (pooled non-Spanish populations; the Spanish subset was 93%/92%). Most useful where TB prevalence is high; cutoff- and population-dependent.
citation.notes: Good rule-in for TB pleuritis in endemic settings; lower pre-test probability reduces PPV. Verify before teaching use.

tests.condition: Asymptomatic, average-risk adults screened for colorectal cancer (colonoscopy/follow-up reference standard)

citation.reference: Lee JK, Liles EG, Bent S, Levin TR, Corley DA. Accuracy of fecal immunochemical tests for colorectal cancer: systematic review and meta-analysis. Ann Intern Med. 2014;160(3):171.
citation.sourceQuote: The pooled sensitivity, specificity, positive likelihood ratio, and negative likelihood ratio of FITs for CRC were 0.79 (95% CI, 0.69 to 0.86), 0.94 (CI, 0.92 to 0.95), 13.10 (CI, 10.49 to 16.35), 0.23 (CI, 0.15 to 0.33), respectively, with an overall diagnostic accuracy of 95% (CI, 93% to 97%).
citation.context: 19 studies; FITs for detecting CRC in asymptomatic average-risk adults; sensitivity improves with lower assay cutoff values.
citation.notes: Highly specific, moderately sensitive: a positive FIT meaningfully raises CRC probability (rule-in for further workup), but a single negative FIT does not exclude CRC. Performance is cutoff-dependent.

tests.testName: Non-contrast cranial CT (modern multidetector, within 6 hours of onset)
tests.condition: Neurologically intact adults presenting with thunderclap headache; non-contrast CT (16-slice or greater) performed within 6 hours of headache onset

citation.reference: Dubosh NM, Bellolio MF, Rabinstein AA, Edlow JA. Sensitivity of Early Brain Computed Tomography to Exclude Aneurysmal Subarachnoid Hemorrhage: A Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis. Stroke. 2016;47(3):750-5.
citation.sourceQuote: Overall sensitivity of the CT was 0.987 (95% confidence intervals, 0.971-0.994) and specificity was 0.999 (95% confidence intervals, 0.993-1.0).
citation.context: 5 studies, ~8907 patients; modern-generation multidetector CT within 6 hours of symptom onset in neurologically intact patients.
citation.notes: Time-critical: extremely sensitive (good rule-out) ONLY when performed within 6 hours of headache onset and read by a qualified radiologist. Sensitivity falls substantially (≤90%) beyond 6 hours, where lumbar puncture/CTA is still considered.

tests.condition: Adults with suspected or known CAD; 64-slice CT angiography vs invasive coronary angiography, ≥50% stenosis threshold, patient-based detection

citation.reference: Mowatt G, Cook JA, Hillis GS, Walker S, Fraser C, Jia X, Waugh N. 64-Slice computed tomography angiography in the diagnosis and assessment of coronary artery disease: systematic review and meta-analysis. Heart. 2008;94(11):1386-93.
citation.sourceQuote: In patient-based detection (n = 1286) 64-slice CT pooled sensitivity was 99% (95% credible interval (CrI) 97% to 99%), specificity 89% (95% CrI 83% to 94%), median positive predictive value (PPV) across studies 93% (range 64-100%) and negative predictive value (NPV) 100% (range 86-100%).
citation.context: 28 studies pooled; ≥50% stenosis defines significant CAD; reference standard invasive coronary angiography. Intervals reported are Bayesian credible intervals (CrI), so CI object omitted.
citation.notes: Very high sensitivity and NPV: excellent rule-out test for significant CAD in suspected/uncertain chest pain. Lower specificity means positives often need confirmation with invasive angiography.

tests.condition: Adults with suspected acute cholecystitis; cholescintigraphy vs clinical/surgical/pathological reference standard

citation.reference: Childs DD, Lalwani N, Craven T, Arif H, Morgan M, Anderson M, Fulcher A. A meta-analysis of the performance of ultrasound, hepatobiliary scintigraphy, CT and MRI in the diagnosis of acute cholecystitis. Abdom Radiol (NY). 2023;49(2):384-398.
citation.sourceQuote: Pooled sensitivity and specificity estimates were 69% (confidence limit [CL] 62-76%) and 79% (CL 71-86%) for US, 91% (CL 86-94%) and 63% (CL 51-74%) for cholescintigraphy, 78% (CL 69-84%) and 81% (CL 71-88%) for CT, and 91% (CL 78-97%) and 93% (CL 70-99%) for MRI.
citation.context: 22 studies (2000-2021); cholescintigraphy (HIDA) values reported; reported confidence limits (CL) are 95% intervals.
citation.notes: Most sensitive single modality for acute cholecystitis (good rule-out) but specificity is lower than ultrasound. Often reserved for equivocal ultrasound. Less practical (longer, radiotracer-based) than first-line ultrasound.

tests.condition: Adults with suspected acute cholecystitis; transabdominal ultrasound vs clinical/surgical/pathological reference standard

citation.reference: Childs DD, Lalwani N, Craven T, Arif H, Morgan M, Anderson M, Fulcher A. A meta-analysis of the performance of ultrasound, hepatobiliary scintigraphy, CT and MRI in the diagnosis of acute cholecystitis. Abdom Radiol (NY). 2023;49(2):384-398.
citation.sourceQuote: Pooled sensitivity and specificity estimates were 69% (confidence limit [CL] 62-76%) and 79% (CL 71-86%) for US, 91% (CL 86-94%) and 63% (CL 51-74%) for cholescintigraphy, 78% (CL 69-84%) and 81% (CL 71-88%) for CT, and 91% (CL 78-97%) and 93% (CL 70-99%) for MRI.
citation.context: 22 studies (2000-2021); ultrasound (US) values reported; reported confidence limits (CL) are 95% intervals.
citation.notes: First-line imaging for suspected acute cholecystitis (bedside, no radiation), but in this recent pooled analysis sensitivity was only moderate, so a normal ultrasound does not fully exclude cholecystitis when clinical suspicion is high. Note: older meta-analyses reported higher US sensitivity.

tests.condition: Adults with acute respiratory failure and suspected pneumonia; urinary antigen test vs culture/smear reference methods

citation.reference: Yasuo S, Murata M, Nakagawa N, Kawasaki T, Yoshida T, Ando K, Okamori S, Okada Y. Diagnostic accuracy of urinary antigen tests for pneumococcal pneumonia among patients with acute respiratory failure suspected pneumonia: a systematic review and meta-analysis. BMJ Open. 2022;12(8):e057216.
citation.sourceQuote: The calculated pooled sensitivity and specificity were of 0.66 (95% CI 0.62 to 0.69) and 0.90 (95% CI 0.85 to 0.93), respectively.
citation.context: 30 studies, 12,366 patients; pneumococcal urinary antigen test vs culture/smear in patients with acute respiratory failure suspected of pneumonia.
citation.notes: Specific but only moderately sensitive: a positive result supports pneumococcal etiology (helps target therapy), but a negative test does not rule out pneumococcal pneumonia. Imperfect culture reference standard likely underestimates true specificity.

tests.condition: Patients with suspected stable coronary artery disease; exercise ECG vs invasive coronary angiography (≥50% luminal obstruction)

citation.reference: Nielsen LH, Ortner N, Nørgaard BL, Achenbach S, Leipsic J, Abdulla J. The diagnostic accuracy and outcomes after coronary computed tomography angiography vs. conventional functional testing in patients with stable angina pectoris: a systematic review and meta-analysis. Eur Heart J Cardiovasc Imaging. 2014;15(9):961-71.
citation.sourceQuote: The per-patient sensitivity [95% confidence interval (95% CI)] to identify significant CAD was 98% (93-99%) for coronary CTA vs. 67% (54-78%) (P < 0.001) for XECG and 99% (96-100%) vs. 73% (59-83%) (P = 0.001) for SPECT. The specificity (95% CI) of coronary CTA was 82% (63-93%) vs. 46% (30-64%) (P < 0.001) for XECG and 71% (60-80%) vs. 48% (31-64%) (P = 0.14) for SPECT.
citation.context: 11 studies (N=1575); exercise ECG (XECG) per-patient values extracted; reference standard invasive coronary angiography ≥50% obstruction.
citation.notes: Modest sensitivity and low specificity: exercise ECG alone is a weak rule-in/rule-out test for anatomic CAD, which is why anatomic (CTA) or functional imaging often outperforms it. Best interpreted with pretest probability.

tests.condition: Adults evaluated for lower-extremity PAD; ABI ≤ 0.90 vs angiography

citation.reference: Xu D, Zou L, Xing Y, Hou L, Wei Y, Zhang J, Qiao Y, Hu D, Xu Y, Li J, Ma Y. Diagnostic value of ankle-brachial index in peripheral arterial disease: a meta-analysis. Can J Cardiol. 2012;29(4):492-8.
citation.sourceQuote: The pooled sensitivity and specificity of ABI ≤ 0.90 for PAD diagnosis were 75% and 86% and the pooled PLR and NLR were 4.18 and 0.29, respectively.
citation.context: 4 studies, 569 patients (922 limbs); ABI ≤ 0.90 threshold vs angiography reference standard. Abstract gives no 95% CI for sensitivity/specificity, so CI object omitted.
citation.notes: Reasonably specific but only moderately sensitive (a normal ABI does not exclude PAD, especially with medial arterial calcification in diabetes/CKD where ABI may be falsely normal/high). A low ABI is useful to rule in PAD.

tests.condition: Population-based breast cancer screening, women mostly aged 40-75; mammography vs histopathology/follow-up reference standard

citation.reference: Zhu C, Wang L, Du LB, Li J, Zhang J, Dai M, Shi JF. [The accuracy of mammography screening for breast cancer: a Meta-analysis]. Zhonghua Liu Xing Bing Xue Za Zhi. 2016;37(9):1296-1305.
citation.sourceQuote: It was estimated that the pooled sensitivity and specificity were 0.81 (95: 0.77-0.84) and 0.96 (95: 0.94-0.96), respectively.
citation.context: 48 studies, 8,551,873 individuals; population-based screening mammography. '95:' in the source denotes the 95% CI. Sensitivity is lower in dense breasts (subgroup 0.74).
citation.notes: High specificity and good sensitivity in average-risk screening, but sensitivity drops in dense breasts. A negative mammogram reduces but does not eliminate the possibility of cancer in symptomatic or high-risk women.

tests.condition: Men in a hospital setting evaluated for prostate cancer; PSA cutoff below 4 ng/mL vs biopsy reference standard

citation.reference: Jin Y, Jung JH, Han WK, Hwang EC, Nho Y, Lee N, Yun JE, Lee KS, Lee SH, Lee H, Yu SY. Diagnostic accuracy of prostate-specific antigen below 4 ng/mL as a cutoff for diagnosing prostate cancer in a hospital setting: A systematic review and meta-analysis. Investig Clin Urol. 2022;63(3):251-261.
citation.sourceQuote: The meta-analysis showed a sensitivity of 0.92 and a specificity of 0.16 for a PSA cutoff below 4 ng/mL.
citation.context: 11 studies; PSA cutoff of 4 ng/mL in a hospital (not population-screening) setting; biopsy reference standard. Abstract gives no 95% CI for this pooled pair, so CI object omitted.
citation.notes: Teaching point: at the classic 4 ng/mL cutoff PSA is sensitive but very non-specific in a referred hospital population (many benign causes elevate PSA), so a raised PSA poorly discriminates cancer on its own. PSA is best used with other risk tools, not as a stand-alone rule-in test.

tests.condition: Patients with known or suspected rheumatoid arthritis; IgM rheumatoid factor vs clinical RA diagnosis

citation.reference: Nishimura K, Sugiyama D, Kogata Y, Tsuji G, Nakazawa T, Kawano S, Saigo K, Morinobu A, Koshiba M, Kuntz KM, Kamae I, Kumagai S. Meta-analysis: diagnostic accuracy of anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide antibody and rheumatoid factor for rheumatoid arthritis. Ann Intern Med. 2007;146(11):797-808.
citation.sourceQuote: The pooled sensitivity, specificity, and positive and negative likelihood ratios for anti-CCP antibody were 67% (95% CI, 62% to 72%), 95% (CI, 94% to 97%), 12.46 (CI, 9.72 to 15.98), and 0.36 (CI, 0.31 to 0.42), respectively. For IgM RF, the values were 69% (CI, 65% to 73%), 85% (CI, 82% to 88%), 4.86 (CI, 3.95 to 5.97), and 0.38 (CI, 0.33 to 0.44).
citation.context: 50 RF studies; values are for IgM RF (sens 69%, spec 85%). The first sentence is quoted to establish the value order (sensitivity, specificity, +LR, -LR) for the IgM RF sentence that follows.
citation.notes: Moderately sensitive and specific: less specific than anti-CCP (95%), so RF is more prone to false positives (also seen in hepatitis C, Sjögren, healthy elderly). Anti-CCP is preferred when ruling in RA; RF and anti-CCP are often used together.

tests.condition: Adults assessed for internal carotid artery stenosis; color duplex peak systolic velocity ≥200 cm/s vs catheter angiography (NASCET method), ≥70% stenosis

citation.reference: Jahromi AS, Cinà CS, Liu Y, Clase CM. Sensitivity and specificity of color duplex ultrasound measurement in the estimation of internal carotid artery stenosis: a systematic review and meta-analysis. J Vasc Surg. 2005;41(6):962-72.
citation.sourceQuote: For the diagnosis of angiographic stenosis of >/=70%, a peak systolic velocity >/=200 cm/s has a sensitivity of 90% (95% CI, 84% to 94%) and a specificity of 94% (95% CI, 88% to 97%).
citation.context: Color duplex PSV ≥200 cm/s threshold for ≥70% internal carotid stenosis; reference standard catheter angiography (NASCET). Measurement properties vary widely between laboratories.
citation.notes: Good sensitivity and specificity for high-grade (≥70%) carotid stenosis, supporting duplex as the first-line screen. Inter-laboratory variability is substantial, so confirmatory CTA/MRA is often obtained before surgery.

tests.condition: Patients with clinically isolated syndrome or suspected MS; CSF oligoclonal IgG bands discriminating CIS/MS from control subjects

citation.reference: Hegen H, Walde J, Berek K, Arrambide G, Gnanapavan S, Kaplan B, Khalil M, Saadeh R, Teunissen C, Tumani H, Villar LM, Willrich MAV, Zetterberg H, Deisenhammer F. Cerebrospinal fluid kappa free light chains for the diagnosis of multiple sclerosis: A systematic review and meta-analysis. Mult Scler. 2022;29(2):169-181.
citation.sourceQuote: Sensitivity and specificity ranged from 52% to 100% (weighted average: 88%) and 69% to 100% (89%) for κ-FLC index and from 37% to 100% (85%) and 74% to 100% (92%) for OCB.
citation.context: 32 studies; values used are the weighted-average sensitivity (85%) and specificity (92%) for oligoclonal bands (OCB) discriminating CIS/MS from controls. Wide between-study heterogeneity; abstract reports weighted averages, not a single pooled point estimate with CI, so CI object omitted.
citation.notes: Specific supporting test for MS (helps rule in/demonstrate intrathecal IgG synthesis) with good sensitivity, but neither sensitive nor specific enough to diagnose MS alone; interpreted within the McDonald criteria and clinical/MRI context.

sequences.name: DVT: D-dimer → compression US
sequences.stopNote: In low pre-test probability, a NEGATIVE D-dimer typically excludes DVT; a positive D-dimer is followed by compression ultrasonography.
sequences.independenceNote: D-dimer (fibrin turnover) and ultrasonography (direct visualization) probe different biology, so conditional independence is a reasonable teaching approximation.

sequences.name: PE: D-dimer → CTPA
sequences.stopNote: In low/intermediate pre-test probability, a NEGATIVE high-sensitivity D-dimer typically ends the workup (PE excluded). CTPA is pursued when the D-dimer is positive or pre-test probability is high.
sequences.independenceNote: D-dimer (a biomarker of fibrin turnover) and CTPA (direct imaging) probe different biology, so treating them as conditionally independent is a reasonable teaching approximation.
